title: Plant Spacing Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Find optimal spacing distances for your garden vegetables. Free calculator for tomatoes, lettuce, carrots, potatoes & more.
intro: The Plant Spacing Calculator tells you the exact recommended distance between individual vegetable plants—and between rows—based on the crop species you're growing. Proper spacing is determined by each plant's mature canopy width, root spread, and light requirements. The core principle: **in-row spacing ≈ mature plant width × 1.0–1.2**, while row spacing ≈ plant height × 0.75–1.0. Get it wrong and you risk overcrowding (poor airflow → fungal disease), or wasted bed space. Use this tool when planning a new garden bed, transplanting seedlings, or converting a traditional row garden to a raised-bed or square-foot layout.
keyTakeaway: **In-row spacing = mature plant diameter × 1.0–1.2; e.g., Tomato indeterminate → 60–80 cm apart, Carrot → 5–8 cm, Lettuce → 20–30 cm. Row spacing is typically 1.25× in-row spacing.**
seoKeywords: how far apart to plant vegetables
useCases: Planning a raised bed to maximize yield per square foot without overcrowding tomatoes, peppers, or squash
Converting a traditional single-row vegetable garden to a denser square-foot or block-planting layout
Calculating seed packet quantities before purchasing: knowing spacing lets you compute exactly how many plants fit your bed dimensions
Timing thinning operations for direct-seeded crops like carrots, beets, and radishes after germination
Designing a companion-planting layout where spacing between species must accommodate both plants' canopy widths simultaneously
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

Plant spacing is not arbitrary—it's derived from each crop's **mature canopy diameter** and **root competition radius**. The general formulas used by cooperative extension services are:

```
In-Row Spacing (cm) = Mature Plant Width (cm) × 1.0 to 1.2
Row Spacing (cm)    = Plant Height (cm) × 0.75 to 1.0
Plants per m²       = 10,000 cm² ÷ (In-Row Spacing × Row Spacing)
Square-Foot Grid    = 30.48 cm ÷ In-Row Spacing  → plants per square foot side
```

For **square-foot gardening (SFG)**, the simplified rule divides a 30 cm × 30 cm grid:
- 1 plant/sq ft → spacing = 30 cm (e.g., peppers, eggplant)
- 4 plants/sq ft → spacing = 15 cm (e.g., lettuce, Swiss chard)
- 9 plants/sq ft → spacing = 10 cm (e.g., spinach, beets)
- 16 plants/sq ft → spacing = 7.5 cm (e.g., carrots, radishes)

---

## Reference Table

| Vegetable | In-Row Spacing | Row Spacing | Plants/m² | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tomato (indeterminate) | 60–80 cm | 90–120 cm | 1–1.5 | Needs caging/staking |
| Tomato (determinate) | 45–60 cm | 75–90 cm | 2–3 | Bush type, self-supporting |
| Pepper (bell/hot) | 40–50 cm | 60–75 cm | 3–4 | Same family, similar needs |
| Eggplant | 45–60 cm | 75–90 cm | 2–3 | Needs full sun, deep root |
| Zucchini / Summer Squash | 60–90 cm | 90–120 cm | 1 | Sprawling vine |
| Winter Squash / Pumpkin | 90–120 cm | 150–180 cm | 0.3–0.5 | Large vine, needs room |
| Cucumber | 30–45 cm | 90–120 cm | 2–3 | Trellis = narrower spacing |
| Broccoli | 45–60 cm | 60–90 cm | 2–3 | Large head requires space |
| Cauliflower | 45–60 cm | 60–90 cm | 2–3 | Same as broccoli |
| Cabbage | 40–50 cm | 60–75 cm | 3–4 | Head size varies by variety |
| Lettuce (head) | 25–30 cm | 30–38 cm | 9–11 | Loose-leaf: 15–20 cm |
| Spinach | 8–15 cm | 30–38 cm | 18–25 | Thin to 15 cm at maturity |
| Carrot | 5–8 cm | 30–38 cm | 33–67 | Thin from ~2.5 cm seedlings |
| Beet | 8–10 cm | 30–38 cm | 26–42 | Each "seed" = cluster |
| Radish | 5–8 cm | 20–25 cm | 50–100 | Fastest: 22–30 days |
| Onion (bulb) | 8–10 cm | 30–38 cm | 26–42 | Sets vs. seeds same spacing |
| Garlic | 10–15 cm | 30 cm | 22–33 | Plant cloves point-up |
| Pea (bush) | 5–8 cm | 45–60 cm | 22–33 | Inoculate seed for N-fixation |
| Pea (climbing) | 5–8 cm | 60–90 cm | 17–25 | Provide trellis ≥1.2 m tall |
| Bean (bush) | 8–10 cm | 45–60 cm | 17–25 | Direct sow, no transplant |
| Bean (pole) | 15–20 cm | 90 cm | 5–7 | Groups of 3–4 at each stake |
| Sweet Corn | 25–35 cm | 75–90 cm | 3–5 | Plant in blocks ≥4 rows for pollination |
| Potato | 25–35 cm | 75–90 cm | 3–5 | Plant seed potato 10–15 cm deep |
| Sweet Potato | 30–45 cm | 90–120 cm | 2–3 | Trailing vine in warm climates |
| Basil | 20–30 cm | 30–45 cm | 7–11 | Pinch flowers to extend harvest |

*Source: USDA NRCS Plant Guides; University Cooperative Extension spacing tables.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Tomatoes in a 4 ft × 8 ft raised bed
A standard 120 cm × 240 cm raised bed with indeterminate tomatoes spaced 75 cm apart fits:
- Along the 240 cm length: floor(240 ÷ 75) = **3 plants**
- Along the 120 cm width: only **1 row** fits (75 cm plant + 45 cm edge buffer)
- **Total: 3 indeterminate tomatoes** per 4×8 bed — any more risks disease and yield loss.

### Case 2 — Mixed salad greens in a 1 m² block
Using block planting at 20 cm spacing:
- 5 columns × 5 rows = **25 lettuce plants per m²**
- Intercropped with radishes at 6 cm in between = radishes harvested in 25 days, clearing space before lettuce needs it. Classic "catch crop" technique.

### Case 3 — Sweet corn pollination block
Corn is wind-pollinated. A single row of 10 plants almost always produces poorly-filled ears. Minimum block: **4 rows × 4 plants** = 16 plants. At 30 cm in-row × 75 cm row spacing, that requires a 1.2 m × 3 m area. Every gardener planting corn should calculate this before buying seeds.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Using in-row spacing as row spacing** — The row-to-row distance is always wider than the in-row spacing (typically 1.25–1.5×) because most vegetables are taller than they are wide. Planting tomatoes at 60 cm × 60 cm instead of 60 cm × 90 cm creates a tunnel that traps moisture and causes early blight.

2. **Ignoring variety within a species** — "Tomato" spacing varies from 30 cm (dwarf patio types) to 90 cm+ (indeterminate heirlooms). Always look up the specific variety, not just the genus. The USDA's ARS plant database distinguishes hundreds of cultivars.

3. **Not accounting for trellising** — Cucumbers on a trellis can be spaced 30 cm apart; sprawling on the ground they need 60–90 cm. The calculator defaults to ground-grown unless noted. A trellis can effectively double planting density.

4. **Skipping thinning on direct-sown crops** — Carrots are sown densely (seeds every 1–2 cm) and *must* be thinned to 5–8 cm after germination. Gardeners who skip thinning get forked, stunted roots. Thinning IS the spacing step for root vegetables.

5. **Applying garden-center pot spacing, not field spacing** — A nursery pot tag may say "space 12 inches apart" for ornamental use, but productive vegetable gardens often use tighter block spacing to shade out weeds and maximize yields. The two systems are intentionally different.

---

faq.q: What is the correct spacing for tomatoes in a raised bed?
faq.a: Indeterminate (vining) tomatoes need 60–80 cm between plants and 90–120 cm between rows. Determinate (bush) varieties can be planted 45–60 cm apart with 75–90 cm between rows. In a typical 4 ft × 8 ft (120 cm × 240 cm) raised bed, you should plant no more than 2–3 indeterminate tomatoes to allow adequate airflow and prevent early blight.

faq.q: How do I calculate how many plants fit in my garden bed?
faq.a: Divide each bed dimension by the in-row spacing: Plants per row = floor(bed length ÷ in-row spacing). Number of rows = floor(bed width ÷ row spacing). Total plants = plants per row × number of rows. For example, a 2 m × 4 m bed with lettuce at 25 cm in-row and 30 cm row spacing: 16 plants × 6 rows = 96 lettuce plants.

faq.q: What is square-foot gardening spacing and how does it differ from traditional spacing?
faq.a: Square-foot gardening (SFG), popularized by Mel Bartholomew, divides beds into 30 cm × 30 cm squares. Each square holds 1, 4, 9, or 16 plants depending on size: 1 for large plants (tomatoes, peppers), 4 for medium (lettuce, chard), 9 for small (spinach, beets), and 16 for tiny (carrots, radishes). SFG spacing is typically 20–30% denser than traditional row gardening because it assumes loose, deeply amended soil with no foot-traffic compaction.

faq.q: Does plant spacing change for container or pot gardening?
faq.a: Yes. In containers, root volume is limited, so you typically use the minimum of the recommended range. A 5-gallon pot (≈19 L) supports 1 pepper or 1 determinate tomato. A half-barrel (≈110 L) can hold 3–4 lettuce plants. The critical factor is the pot's diameter relative to the plant's mature root spread—most vegetables need at least 30 cm of soil depth and 20–30 cm of lateral root room per plant.

faq.q: Why does sweet corn require block planting instead of a single row?
faq.a: Sweet corn (Zea mays) is wind-pollinated: pollen from tassels (top) must fall onto silks (sides). A single row means most pollen blows away before reaching silks, resulting in ears with missing kernels. The USDA recommends a minimum block of 4 rows × 4 plants (16 plants total) for reliable pollination. Wider blocks (4+ rows) dramatically improve fill rates, especially in low-wind gardens.

faq.q: How does spacing affect disease risk in vegetables?
faq.a: Tight spacing reduces airflow between plants, keeping foliage wet longer after rain or irrigation. Fungal diseases like early blight (Alternaria solani) on tomatoes, powdery mildew on squash, and downy mildew on lettuce all proliferate in humid, stagnant-air environments. University extension research shows that increasing tomato spacing from 45 cm to 75 cm can reduce foliar disease incidence by 30–50% without significant yield loss per plant.

faq.q: What is the minimum spacing for carrots, and how should I thin them?
faq.a: Carrot seeds are sown 1–2 cm apart and thinned in two stages: first to 2.5 cm when seedlings are 5 cm tall, then to the final 5–8 cm when they reach 10 cm tall. Skipping thinning causes roots to fork or remain pencil-thin. Use scissors to snip (not pull) thinnings at soil level to avoid disturbing neighboring seedling roots. Final in-row spacing of 5 cm yields standard carrots; 8 cm yields jumbo storage roots.

faq.q: How do I adjust spacing for intensive or biointensive gardening methods?
faq.a: Biointensive gardening (developed by John Jeavons at Ecology Action) plants vegetables at 'equidistant spacing'—the distance at which mature leaves just touch neighboring plants. This is typically 60–75% of traditional row spacing. For example, standard cabbage is planted at 45 cm; biointensive spacing is 38 cm. The method requires deeply dug (60 cm+), heavily composted soil to prevent root competition at these tighter densities.

sources.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guides – Vegetable Crop Spacing
